name: Noah
shortDescription: Noah (Nuh) is a prophet in Islam, known for building an ark to save believers from a great flood sent by God as punishment.
description: Noah (Nuh in Arabic) is a revered prophet in Islam, considered one of the most important figures in Islamic history. He is mentioned extensively in the Quran and Islamic tradition, and his story parallels the biblical account of Noah. According to Islamic belief, Noah was chosen by Allah to warn his people, who had become deeply corrupted by idolatry and wickedness. Despite preaching for many years, only a small number of people believed in his message. Allah then instructed Noah to build an ark to save himself, his family, and pairs of animals from a devastating flood that would engulf the earth as punishment for the disbelievers. The flood is described as a cataclysmic event, with rain pouring down incessantly and the earth erupting with water. After the flood subsided, Noah and his followers disembarked from the ark, and a new era began, with Noah and his descendants repopulating the earth. Noah is considered a righteous and patient prophet, a model of faith and obedience to God. His story serves as a reminder of the consequences of disbelief and the importance of adhering to divine guidance.

quests.title: The Call to Prophecy
quests.description: Allah commands Noah to warn his people of their wickedness and the impending flood if they do not repent. Noah dedicates his life to preaching, facing ridicule and rejection for centuries.
quests.outcome: Despite his tireless efforts, only a small number of people believe in him.

quests.title: Building the Ark
quests.description: Allah instructs Noah to build an ark of specific dimensions, a task that seems impossible given the resources and Noah's age. The disbelievers mock him relentlessly as he toils.
quests.outcome: Noah, with the help of believers, completes the ark according to Allah's instructions.

quests.title: Gathering the Creatures
quests.description: As the flood approaches, Allah commands Noah to gather a pair of every animal species onto the ark. Noah struggles to accomplish this miraculous task.
quests.outcome: Pairs of animals, birds, and insects miraculously arrive and board the ark.

quests.title: The Great Flood
quests.description: The heavens open and the earth erupts, unleashing a devastating flood. Noah and his companions seek refuge in the ark as the waters rise, engulfing everything.
quests.outcome: The ark floats on the floodwaters for many days, protecting Noah, his family, believers, and the animals from complete destruction.

quests.title: Mount Judi and New Beginnings
quests.description: After the flood subsides, Allah commands the earth to absorb the waters and the heavens to cease raining. The ark comes to rest on Mount Judi.
quests.outcome: Noah, his family, and the believers disembark from the ark, establishing a new civilization on earth, grateful for Allah's mercy.

worshipPractices.name: Supplication for Protection
worshipPractices.description: Muslims often invoke Noah's name when seeking protection from calamities and natural disasters, drawing inspiration from his faith and Allah's intervention during the Great Flood. This supplication serves as a reminder of divine power and the importance of steadfast belief in the face of adversity. Reciting specific verses from the Quran related to Noah's story is also common during times of hardship.

festivals.name: No specific festival
festivals.description: While there isn't a specific festival dedicated solely to Noah (Nuh), his story is often recounted and reflected upon during Islamic holidays like Eid al-Adha and religious lectures throughout the year. These occasions provide opportunities to learn from his unwavering faith, patience, and dedication to Allah's message in the face of widespread disbelief. His narrative also serves as a reminder of the importance of repentance and seeking forgiveness.

cultural.modernLegacy: Noah's story continues to inspire contemporary Islamic art and literature, often serving as a metaphor for resilience, faith, and the consequences of moral corruption. His narrative reinforces the Islamic emphasis on monotheism and the importance of heeding divine warnings, shaping ethical and moral discussions within Muslim communities. Furthermore, Noah's ark is sometimes used as a symbol of hope and salvation in the face of global challenges.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Manu, like Noah, is warned of a great flood and builds a boat to save himself, his family, and representatives of all living creatures. Both figures are seen as pivotal in repopulating the earth after the deluge.

Deucalion and his wife Pyrrha are the only survivors of a great flood sent by Zeus to punish humanity. Similar to Noah, they repopulate the earth, although through a different method (casting stones over their shoulders).

Utnapishtim is warned by a god (Ea/Enki) of a devastating flood and instructed to build an ark to save himself, his family, and animals. He becomes immortal after the flood, a reward for his obedience and preservation of life, mirroring Noah's status as a righteous figure.

While not the primary focus of a flood myth, Bergelmir and his wife are the only surviving giants of the blood flood caused by Ymir's death. They escape in a wooden box, ensuring the continuation of the giant race, similar to Noah's role in continuing humanity.

associations.name: Dove
associations.significance: The dove symbolizes peace and the end of the flood, bringing back news of dry land after the deluge.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Water represents both destruction (the flood) and purification, cleansing the world of sin.

associations.name: Three
associations.significance: Represents the three sons of Noah, who are seen as the progenitors of different races after the flood.

associations.name: Patience
associations.significance: Noah's unwavering faith and perseverance during the long construction of the ark and the flood highlight the importance of patience in the face of adversity.

associations.name: Olive Branch
associations.significance: Signifies hope and new beginnings after the devastation of the flood, offering a sign of renewed life and divine mercy.

associations.name: Rain
associations.significance: Represents the divine punishment and cleansing that Noah experienced.

associations.name: Divine Covenant
associations.significance: Noah's story represents a covenant between God and humanity, promising safety and a new beginning for those who believe.

feats.title: Enduring Faith in the Face of Adversity
feats.description: Noah remained steadfast in his faith and commitment to Allah's message for 950 years, despite constant ridicule and disbelief from his people. He never wavered from his divine mission.
feats.significance: This demonstrates unwavering faith and patience, serving as an example for Muslims to persevere in the face of hardship.

feats.title: Constructing the Ark
feats.description: Noah, guided by Allah, successfully built a massive ark capable of withstanding a global flood. He utilized his skill and divine guidance to complete the monumental task.
feats.significance: This highlights his obedience to Allah and his ability to overcome seemingly impossible challenges.

feats.title: Saving Humanity and Animal Life
feats.description: By boarding the ark with believers and pairs of animals, Noah preserved life on Earth during the Great Flood. He ensured the continuation of both human and animal species.
feats.significance: This act of preservation highlights his role as a pivotal figure in human history and the natural world, fulfilling Allah's will.

feats.title: Establishing a New Civilization
feats.description: After the flood, Noah and his descendants repopulated the Earth, establishing a new civilization based on faith and righteousness. His lineage became the ancestors of many future prophets and nations.
feats.significance: This marks the beginning of a new era for humanity, emphasizing the importance of faith and obedience in building a just and prosperous society.

companions.name: Ham
companions.description: One of Noah's sons who embarked on the Ark. He is considered a believer in the message of Noah.

companions.name: Shem
companions.description: Another of Noah's sons who boarded the Ark. He is also considered a believer and righteous follower.

companions.name: Japheth
companions.description: The third of Noah's sons who joined him on the Ark. Like his brothers, he is seen as a believer who was saved from the flood.

companions.name: The Believing Animals
companions.description: Pairs of animals from every species who were chosen by Allah to be saved on the Ark. They represent the continuation of life on Earth after the flood.

companions.name: The Ark
companions.description: The divinely constructed vessel that carried Noah, his family, and the animals. It served as their refuge and means of survival during the great flood.

companions.name: Angel Jibril (Gabriel)
companions.description: Jibril, the archangel, delivered Allah's messages and guidance to Noah. He provided divine instructions regarding the construction of the Ark and other matters.

extendedContent.title: Early Life & Prophetic Call
extendedContent.content:
In Islamic tradition, Noah (Nuh) is depicted as a righteous man born into a society consumed by idolatry and moral decay. While the Quran doesn't provide extensive details about his early life, commentaries and historical accounts elaborate on his lineage and upbringing. He is often described as a descendant of Adam, maintaining a pure line of faith amidst widespread disbelief. Unlike the biblical account, Islamic sources do not explicitly mention Noah's occupation before his prophethood, but emphasize his inherent goodness and wisdom, which set him apart from his peers.

The Quran highlights that Allah chose Noah as a prophet and tasked him with delivering a message of monotheism (Tawhid) to his people. Surah Nuh details the core of his prophetic call: to warn his people against worshipping idols and to submit to the one true God, Allah. This call was met with resistance and mockery, as the people were deeply entrenched in their ancestral beliefs and refused to abandon their idols. Islamic scholars note that Noah's patience and perseverance during this period are exemplary, reflecting the qualities of a true prophet. His unwavering faith in Allah served as a beacon of hope and guidance in a time of profound spiritual darkness.

Further, it is noted that the people challenged him to bring forth the punishment he warned them of and that the people of Noah were the first people to have idols, these idols are named in the Quran as Wadd, Suwa, Yaghuth, Ya'uq and Nasr.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Challenges: The Flood and Resistance
extendedContent.content:
Noah's greatest challenge was undoubtedly the overwhelming resistance he faced from his people. The Quran recounts his tireless efforts to preach monotheism for 950 years (Surah Al-Ankabut, 29:14), yet only a small number of followers believed in him. The majority, including his own son (in some interpretations), rejected his message and persisted in their idolatry and wickedness. This rejection was not merely passive; they actively mocked and persecuted Noah and his followers, attempting to undermine his mission and discourage others from accepting his message. The Quran describes their arrogance and defiance, highlighting their disbelief in the divine punishment Noah warned them about.

The culmination of this resistance was the Great Flood, a cataclysmic event sent by Allah as a consequence of their persistent disobedience. The Quran describes the flood as a divine decree, a cleansing of the earth from corruption and disbelief. Noah was commanded to build an Ark and to take pairs of every creature, as well as his believing family, to safety. The flood itself was a trial of immense proportions, testing Noah's faith and resilience. The rising waters symbolized the overwhelming power of Allah and the futility of resisting His will. The separation between the believers and the disbelievers became starkly evident, with the flood serving as a definitive judgment.

Islamic scholars interpret the flood not just as a physical event, but also as a symbolic representation of the purification of the earth and the triumph of good over evil. The survival of Noah and his Ark signifies the preservation of faith and the continuation of God's message through a chosen few. The Quran emphasizes that the flood was a sign (ayah) for humanity, a reminder of the consequences of disobedience and the importance of submitting to Allah's will. The ark itself is often regarded as a divine miracle and a symbol of divine protection for the believers.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence: A Prophet of Resilience
extendedContent.content:
Noah's legacy in Islam is profound, extending far beyond the narrative of the flood. He is regarded as one of the most important prophets (Ulul Azm min ar-Rusul), a select group distinguished by their unwavering patience and resilience in the face of adversity. His story serves as a timeless example of faith, perseverance, and reliance on Allah in the face of overwhelming challenges. Noah's unwavering commitment to his mission, despite facing ridicule and rejection for centuries, inspires Muslims to remain steadfast in their beliefs and to continue striving for righteousness, even when faced with seemingly insurmountable obstacles.

The Quran highlights Noah as a role model for all believers. His prayers, his supplications to Allah, and his unwavering trust in divine guidance are frequently cited as examples of proper conduct and devotion. The story of Noah is used to teach lessons about the importance of monotheism, the consequences of disbelief, and the power of repentance. His narrative reinforces the Islamic belief in divine justice and the ultimate triumph of good over evil. The emphasis on his long life of preaching underscores the importance of consistent and persistent efforts in spreading the message of Islam.

Furthermore, Noah is considered a pivotal figure in the genealogy of prophets. Islamic tradition links his descendants to many subsequent prophets, solidifying his position as a patriarch and a foundational figure in the Abrahamic lineage. This connection reinforces the continuity of the prophetic message and the interconnectedness of the Abrahamic faiths, emphasizing the shared values of monotheism, righteousness, and submission to God.

extendedContent.title: Death & Afterlife
extendedContent.content:
Islamic sources do not provide extensive details regarding the specific circumstances of Noah's death. The Quran focuses primarily on his prophetic mission and the events surrounding the flood. However, it is understood that Noah lived a long life, as indicated by the Quranic verse mentioning his 950 years of preaching. It is believed that he eventually passed away peacefully after fulfilling his prophetic duty and witnessing the establishment of a new generation committed to monotheism. The exact location of his burial is not definitively stated in the Quran or authentic Hadith, but various traditions and local legends place his tomb in different locations, including Najaf in Iraq, and Baalbek in Lebanon.

In Islamic belief, all prophets, including Noah, are honored and blessed by Allah. They are considered to be among the most righteous and exalted individuals in human history. While the Quran doesn't explicitly describe Noah's afterlife in detail, it is understood that he resides in Paradise (Jannah) with other prophets and righteous believers. The Quran assures believers that those who have faith and do good deeds will be rewarded with eternal bliss in the afterlife. Noah's unwavering faith and dedication to Allah certainly qualify him for such a reward.

Furthermore, the story of Noah serves as a reminder of the ultimate accountability that awaits all individuals on the Day of Judgment. Those who rejected his message and persisted in their disbelief faced a severe punishment in this world, and they will also face a reckoning in the afterlife. The story of Noah, therefore, serves as a powerful reminder of the importance of faith, righteousness, and submission to Allah's will, both in this life and in the Hereafter.

achievements: Building the Ark under divine guidance
Saving believers and animal pairs from the Great Flood
Preaching monotheism for centuries despite rejection
Surviving the flood and disembarking safely
Re-establishing life on Earth after the Deluge
subtitle: A Prophet of God who built the Ark
